articles.title: About Me
articles.description: As part of my degree requirement, I have been developing my quantitative analysis skills through R and I will be showing my progress in my final year project - a policy report - by producing a regression table. The activities I have done in previous assignments will demonstrate the development of my skills.

            Bio
            Hi, my name is Natalie Annett and I am a 3rd year
            Politics and International Relations student, with a
            specific interest in geopolitics. I enjoy reading in my
            spare time outside of my academic responsibilities and I
            have recently found interest in philosophical books.
            
            
            Education
            University of Essex | Colchester, UK
            BSc Politics and International Relations | October 2022 -

articles.title: Portfolio
articles.contents:
##GV300 Progress
This is what I have been doing in my previous assignments. Below you will see that I will load the packages needed for the code, as well as the codes that will be essential for the display of the data visualisation.


knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)

library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)


After this, I will demonstrate the code that I needed in order to load the data. In this case the dataset is from the Parliament website and it details voter turnout. The data visualisation I intend to show is the turnout between the different devolved nations.


uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% tolower() %>% str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")


Below here is the code required to form the code to make that data visualisation.


uk1 <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`)
uk2 <- uk1 %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country      = country_name,
    valid_votes  = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate   = electorate
  )
uk3 <- uk2 %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate  = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
uk <- uk3 %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)

overall_mean <- mean(uk$valid_votes)


Here is the code that, using the ggplot2 package, that will create the visualisation demonstrating the turnout for the 4 UK devolved nations.


overall_mean <- mean(uk$valid_votes)

ggplot(uk, aes(x = valid_votes)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "#008080", colour = "black", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = overall_mean, 
             color = "#FF2050", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, ncol = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Sizes by Country",
    subtitle = "Red line shows overall UK mean",
    x = "Number of Valid Votes",
    y = "Count of Constituencies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )
